title: Chapter 42: Intelligence
content: *wren. day fifteen. the frequency.*
She found him in the noise.

It happened on the third morning of listening—really listening, the kind that left her temples throbbing and her vision streaked with afterimages. Wren sat in the operations room with her back against the server rack, eyes closed, palms flat on the cold floor. The concrete conducted vibration poorly, but the data streams didn't need a medium. They existed in the air itself, a constant electromagnetic hum that most human brains filtered out as background static.
Her brain didn't filter anymore.

She'd been sorting the frequencies like a radio operator scanning bands. Emergency services on the low end—predictable, repetitive, easy to ignore. Commercial traffic in the middle range—chatter and noise, the ceaseless economy of desire. And above it all, tight and encrypted, NeuroScale's private network. She'd been avoiding it. The encryption gave her headaches, a shrill dissonance that pressed behind her eyeballs like ice picks.
But this morning she pushed through.

The pain arrived in layers. First pressure, then heat, then a high keening note that seemed to bypass her ears entirely and vibrate in the roots of her teeth. She tasted metal. Her nose began to bleed—she felt the warmth on her upper lip but didn't move to wipe it. She pressed deeper.

The encryption wasn't a wall. It was a language she didn't speak. The data was there, moving through NeuroScale's channels in dense packets, each one wrapped in algorithmic layers that folded in on themselves. She couldn't read the content. But she could hear the structure—the rhythm of the traffic, the patterns of request and response, the architecture of communication.
And in those patterns, she heard something familiar.

It happened every eleven minutes. A specific signal, traveling from an external source to NeuroScale's core servers. The signal carried a frequency she'd heard before—in the safe house, when Ash had pinged their secure channel. His signature. His rhythm. Ash was alive, and his implant was still active, and every eleven minutes the implant transmitted a status ping to NeuroScale's central system.

She followed the signal backward, tracing its path through the encrypted layers. The routing was complex—bounced through three relay stations, stripped of identifiable headers, funneled through a subnet that didn't appear on any public registry. But the destination was clear. The signal terminated at a facility forty-seven kilometers outside the city, in a district called Blackmoss.

Wren opened her eyes. The operations room swam. She grabbed the edge of the console desk and waited for the vertigo to pass. Her nose was bleeding freely now; she used her sleeve again, the fabric stiff with old blood, adding another dark stripe to the collection.
Blackmoss Facility. She'd never heard of it. But she could find it.

She pulled up the tablet and searched public records. Blackmoss appeared as a decommissioned pharmaceutical warehouse—property owned by a holding company, which was owned by another holding company, which was owned by a trust registered in a jurisdiction that didn't require disclosure of beneficiaries. The corporate shell game. Standard NeuroScale playbook.

But satellite imagery was harder to scrub. She found imagery from six months ago showing a compound that looked nothing like a decommissioned warehouse. Guard towers. Reinforced perimeter. A helipad. Vehicle traffic consistent with a population of two to three hundred staff. This was an active facility, and it was large, and it was hidden, and Ash was inside it.

She sat with that knowledge for a long time. The data streams hummed around her—the city's endless chorus, oblivious. Somewhere out there, in a building she'd never seen, Ash was breathing. His heart was beating. His implant was pinging. And every eleven minutes, that ping traveled through the encrypted network and landed in NeuroScale's servers like a homing beacon.

They were tracking him. Of course they were tracking him. The implant was their design, their frequency, their leash. They'd known where he was the moment they captured him. And now, ironically, so did she—because they'd built a system designed to be impenetrable, but they'd built it on frequencies that she could hear.
Encryption was a lock on a door. There was no frequency that could be entirely silenced. And she had learned to hear the shape of the lock itself.

The morning deepened. Wren ate nothing. She drank cold water and pressed her palms to the floor and listened again—this time not for Ash but for the broader traffic on NeuroScale's network. She couldn't decode the content, but she could read the metadata through rhythm and volume. Which channels were busiest. Which protocols were active. Which communications carried urgency.
She found it by midday.

A surge of traffic on a specific subnet—dense, frantic, structured like preparation. The frequency of the packets suggested logistics: supply chains, schedules, deployment orders. She couldn't read the words, but she could feel the momentum. Something was accelerating. NeuroScale was ramping up.
She needed context. Wren pulled up public feeds on the tablet and searched for NeuroScale news. The results made her stomach tighten.
NEUROSCALE ANNOUNCES LAUNCH DATE FOR ANX-00

The headline was everywhere. Business feeds. Health feeds. Science feeds. Every channel carrying the same breathless excitement: NeuroScale's revolutionary anxiety medication, approved after expedited review, would launch in three days. ANX-00. The drug that erased people. Packaged as salvation, wrapped in clinical language, celebrated as a breakthrough.
Three days.

She scrolled through the coverage. Testimonials from trial participants—"I feel like myself for the first time." Expert commentary—"A paradigm shift in psychiatric care." Stock projections—"NeuroScale poised for record valuation." Not a single mention of the subjects. Not a whisper of the thirteen files. The world was about to celebrate a drug that had been tested on unwilling human beings—human beings who had been terminated when the trials concluded.
Petra Volkov. Amara Osei. Jin Hai. Thirteen names. Thirteen lives consumed by the machine and erased from the record. And in three days, the machine would go public.

The data streams hummed. She heard the NeuroScale traffic flowing—confident, rhythmic, the sound of a corporation that believed itself untouchable. And beneath it, every eleven minutes, Ash's implant pinged its steady pulse. Alive. Still alive. Still there.
She had three days.

Wren stood. The room tilted and she gripped the console until the dizziness passed. She walked to the water bottles and drank one dry. The cold helped. The tremor in her hands was persistent now—a fine vibration that wouldn't still. She pressed her palms together and held them until the shaking subsided to something manageable.

Three days to break into a facility she'd never seen. Three days to extract a man from NeuroScale custody. Three days to broadcast thirteen files to a world that didn't know it needed to see them. Three days before ANX-00 launched and the erasure became industrial.
Impossible. Obviously impossible. She was one woman with a glitch in her brain and a library in a dead building and a key she didn't know how to use.
But Ash had stolen the Signal Key for a reason. He'd believed it could amplify a signal beyond suppression. He'd believed that one voice, boosted loud enough, could break through the noise.

She picked up the Signal Key from the desk. It was warm, as always—warm in a way that defied the ambient temperature. She held it in both hands and closed her fingers around it. She couldn't feel its frequency yet, not clearly. But she could feel its potential, coiled tight, waiting.
She needed to unlock it. She needed to understand what it wanted from her.
And then she needed to get to Blackmoss.

The afternoon was spent mapping. She couldn't access NeuroScale's internal schematics—the encryption was too dense for her to parse—but the data streams gave her something almost as valuable. She could hear the traffic patterns, and traffic patterns told stories. She heard the shift changes—rotations of staff moving in and out of the facility every eight hours. She heard the security protocols—authentication challenges that pulsed at regular intervals. She heard the drone activity—steady automated sweeps that transmitted telemetry every ninety seconds.

The perimeter was tight. Three layers of electronic security, each running on a different frequency. The inner facility used biometric locks she couldn't override remotely. The staff rotation meant a consistent population inside, which meant eyes on every corridor. Getting in would be hard. Getting out with Ash would be harder.

But the data streams had already given her one advantage no one at NeuroScale anticipated: she could hear them. Their encrypted communications were invisible to conventional interception, but they weren't silent. And she was learning to listen.

By evening she had a partial map of Blackmoss—not a floor plan, but a traffic map. Which corridors were busiest at which hours. Where the security checkpoints thinned. Where the data streams pooled and concentrated, suggesting server infrastructure. And deep in the center of the facility, a node that transmitted more heavily than anywhere else. That was where Ash's ping terminated. That was where they were keeping him.

She closed the tablet and sat in the dark. The fluorescent tube had finally died; the only light came from the green LEDs on the backup routers, scattered like distant stars. The data streams hummed their endless song. Somewhere in the chorus, Ash's signal pulsed—quiet, steady, patient.
She was coming. He didn't know it yet, but she was coming.
Three days. She had three days to turn a glitch into a weapon and a key into a broadcast tower. Three days to learn the language of a facility designed to be inescapable. Three days to be ready.
The odds were impossible. The time was insufficient. The resources amounted to a dead building and a stolen key and a brain that was rewriting itself in ways she didn't fully understand.
But she could hear him. Every eleven minutes, his signal reached her through the static. And as long as she could hear him, she knew the direction.
Wren lay down in the sleeping bag and stared at the ceiling. The router LEDs blinked. The data streams sang. And in the darkness, she began to plan.
